sentences.text: Yes.
Now we had to organize our life.
In meantime, I got a card from a Polish friend, a friend of my brother's, the youngest brother.
A card which he wrote to me like that, don't expect Marian-- that was his Polish name-- don't expect Marian.
He got so sick that he's not going to come back.
So I knew what it means.
It was the same night he was taken on the way to Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look, you want me to tell this or to shorten it so we can--
Yes.
After a few days, somebody knocks at the door, I open the door, and here stands this Staszek from Krakow.
When he saw that the one in which I suspected--
Yes.
And when he saw me, he went pale like this wall.
You are here?
Couldn't believe it.
So I told him, very innocently, why I should not be here.
And [?
Dwarkus ?] [?
Vyaslav-- ?] that's my brother-in-law-- so I told him that he got a job somewhere in the country, and they left.
So he left.
But a few days later, he rings me.
He wants me to come to a cafe somewhere in the city, and he will tell me what happened to Marian, to my brother.
Well, my aunt was sitting there and I was-- I raised my voice.
I was the wife of an officer, and the mayor Jew is asking me to come to see him in a cafe.
I went on a high horse and told him off.
And I told him, if you want to see me, you know where I live.
And my aunt was so happy that I had acted like that.
He never bothered us again.
But that confirmed my suspicion that he was the one who caused--
He was not alive after the war.
But up till that point he was still alive.
Probably.
I never saw him again.
Apparently, he had the real-- he was really serving the Gestapo.
There were some Jews who did this, so they are.
And they were denouncing other Jews.
Like, for example, my best friend, who was in-- went to Krakow, and the first time she walked in the street, a Jew from shamash recognized her and denounced her.
You see, there were some Jews.
I hate to tell this story, but unluckily there were.
As I told you I gave it to my brother, and that was a basis of his business.
Never saw anything because he-- he, as I told you, he made a lot of money out of it, really a fortune.
And we decided that we are going to rent a flat, and we will live properly there.
But he did not come back.
He did no limb.
He just left me the 5,000 zlotys, which was enough for two dice if you wanted to buy something on the black market.
Otherwise you could not eat.
Yes.
So then we lived like that.
Everyday went to work-- Aunt Paula and Uncle Frank-- and I was cooking and carrying the household.
And as I told you, she was so marvelous.
Not only marvelous, they just-- I had the good luck that they just adored me.
And I-- and the child, they were all over him.
Anyway, I started to help out a little bit.
I was knitting now to earn some money and as well because we were really very tight.
But the fact the money that he-- she, for example, earned from a patient, she bought a beautiful coat for my baby, [LAUGHS] not that this.
Look, of course it was under German occupation.
And the Poles did not have it easy themselves.
First of all, if it comes to food, and secondly, the resistance.
Every time that they did something to the Germans, the-- the Germans were punishing them.

And I was there, for example, once with the baby in the pram when they closed both sides of a street, I just had time enough to run with the pram under this-- how you call this, iron shutters-- under the iron shutter to a shop.
And they closed, and they gathered every men which was in the street, you see, and they send them off.
Most probably they said that is how the son of my Aunt Paula was caught.
They sent them over to Auschwitz or to other camps, or they.
Once I saw, when I walked in the street, a row Polish people hanging from the balcony as a punishment for some deed which they did.
But that did not stop them to hand over the Jews to the German, see?
Anyway, we are at home.
I am living the normal life, going out, going shopping, cooking, and going-- every few days, I had to go to the hospital.
And it was on the other part of Warsaw because I got there a bottle of mother's milk for my baby.
I could not with a pram to go on a train.
I was walking back, like from here, for example, to the-- this-- the bridge and back.
Oh, yes.
And back.
And of course, what I could do, I was-- I had to do it.
And every time when somebody looked at me, I said to myself, he recognize me.
He thinks something.
I-- I-- I have black hair.
It was every time, when I had to go out, I looked at myself in the mirror and thought to myself, if I would have light hair, if I would be taller, if my nose would be shorter.
But I could not change that, so I had to live as I was.
So we lived like that a few months.
Around June, July, we started to hear noises about the uprising is preparing.
In our circles, we heard that more than noises.
But the Russian were advancing, and they were deep into Poland, and they got as far as that part of Warsaw which was over the Vistula.
The Vistula was a dividing Warsaw in two parts.
Prague was like a suburb, a big suburb.
And they stood there.
They did not advance.
We were under the Germans, still they had a very strong grip on us still, and the Russian stood there.
Then the Polish government, in exile, decided that it is time to start fighting because they want to free Warsaw, and not the Russian.
So the first of August 1944 it started.
And it started not far from our house.
To first shot were shot from not far from our house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to go into the cellar because the Germans were throwing at us everything what they had.
And so it was summer, full summer, August, hot summer.
I had, again, my coat, which I had the loose coat on and light sun dress, and I took the baby and the necessities.
We went to the cellar.
And the next day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are there, sitting, and the barrage of bombing and the fire was so intense that, see, we could hear the house trembling.
And I looked around, my aunt is not here.
So I run upstairs, and-- you want me to hear the story because it is a very unusual story?
My aunt is sitting on the bed crying.
So I asked her, why you are crying?
And she told me, I had a dream.
I had a dream that you left to me alone.
You all left and you left me alone in this house burning.
So I told her, you and I, that we would never do it.
We would never go without you.
And you are going with me down.
Then, the first time, as long as I was with her, she asked me a personal question.
She told me, look, Vyaslav-- that my brother-in-law, she didn't know that it is my brother-in-law.
She was always telling that when the war will be over, you have a fortune to come back to.
You know that I will not give-- whatever happens, I will not, I will not give up the child.
The child is mine, she told me.
But we are poor people, and I don't want the child to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without any means to--
So I don't know.
I had such a funny feeling then that I told her everything.
That I am Jewish, that that was my sister, that-- that Henry was my brother.
She was sitting there looking at me.
And then she told me, look it doesn't make any difference to me what you are.
You are my daughter.
And she told me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I had don't want anybody from the family ever know because I don't know how they will react.
And I took her down to the cellar, and she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she promised me that she's not going anywhere.

You know, then this resistance, the members of the resistance, they prepared for the uprising, and they cut a opening through from one cellar to another under the whole Warsaw like a honeycomb so that you did not need to move from one street to another.
You didn't need to go outside.
So we are sitting in the cellar, suddenly she tells me she wants to go and see what her sister is doing just two streets away.
So I told her, but you promise me you don't go anywhere else, only to sister and back.
One hour, two hours, after three hours-- after a few hours, I don't know, we never looked in the cellar, we never watched the hours.
Somebody comes running-- family Prowyczek family Prowyczek-- because I was family, yes, and my uncle was family.
So I had a funny premonition because he came from a different direction.
So I go to-- I run to him, and I ask him-- I gave my baby to somebody, I don't know who was standing there-- and I run to him.
What happened?
So he tells me, on the way a woman started to give birth in a cellar.
And they called her out-- she was a midwife-- she went, and she had to cross the street.
And the first shrapnel tore her to pieces.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we were just waiting for a moment of silence that the German will stop shooting and we went there.
We picked her up, picked up those pieces, and we buried her in the yard of this house.
And in the afternoon, our house started to burn like the house did, and we have to leave the house.
And that was her dream.
We left her in the burning house.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we went to the other cellars.

And then all my neighbors decided-- because they liked her so much, and they respected her so much-- that as I am her niece, you know, they decided that they are going to take care of me and the uncle.
And there were 20 people from all the other flats.
This, they took us together, and wherever they went, they took us which was very good for me.
I was not a lost stranger anymore.
So we started to walk from one cellar to another.
When the German were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taking a street, one street after the other, they were burning and-- this-- the houses very systematically.
And we went from between one to the other.
Of course, I don't remember how we lived.
I remember only that once they caught a horse, a living horse, and they killed it in the-- the-- in the cellar, and we I had raw meat, horse meat.
Even my baby, 14 months old, had to eat raw horse meat.
And once they brought some salted fish and we had to eat it.
There was hardly any water anyway.
After a few-- we did not know if it is day, if it is night.
If it is one week or if it is two weeks.
We don't know.
We just walked.
We were so cramped.
The lice-- you never saw the size of lice.
They were eating us alive.
At night when we were lying down-- if we had the room to lie-- the rats were walking all over us.
But we were alive.
And we were walking till we lost all account where we are.
You see, we-- so many times we-- I did not know Warsaw.
For me, it was all the same, but those people who knew Warsaw, we just lost account where we are.
So one day, it was a little bit again silence.
And so I took the baby out and sit in front of the house.
I don't know where it was, somewhere in the middle of Warsaw, in the center of Warsaw.
And then comes along and woman from shamash, a Jewish young.
I knew her.
She was living in this house where we were then in the cellar.
Of course, she did not recognize me.
I went back to the cellar, a few minutes later she comes back with some rolls, with some of this.
And she started to feed all the babies.
One of my neighbors had a baby girl the same day as I had my son.
So she started to feed everybody.
And she left.
The minute she left, my neighbors tell like that, what is Jewish is doing still here?
In this condition that we were, they had still in their mind, what this Jewish is doing here.
They did not thank her that she brought them food.
Anyway, that was that.
We stayed like that two months into cellar.
Still, there was not even standing room because the Germans started to squeeze us and squeeze us and more and more people started to come into the same center.
After two months, they started to throw leaflets down for the civilian to come out.
That they will not to do anything to the civilian and to come out.
We did not have any choice.
We did not have anywhere.
The children, especially, were dying around us.
Anything, there was no food, no water, no nothing.
So we came out.
We came out, we did not knew where we are because everything around us did not exist.
Warsaw did not exist.
There were piles of bricks, burnt out shells.
And that was nobody knew where we are.
So they took us to the railway station, and then we had to wait for transport.
So we're standing, it was night till we come there.
We went over all of those rubbish, rubble and [INAUDIBLE].
It was night, and we are standing, so I protested, we did not know where we are going, what they are going to do with us.
Nobody ask us if we want a drop of water or something like that.
And but for a change, the Russians started to bombard us.
You see, because they started to bombard right away in the railway line, and they could not know that we are standing there.
They could not care less, anyway, that we are standing there.
Anyway, we got to a train, and they brought us to camp, a transit camp.
They call it camp.
That was the old abattoirs.
People were lying there for weeks on cement floor, still stained with blood of the animals, sick and dying, and nobody even looked at them.
But my group, as I told you, they were very enterprising people.
They come from the poor side of the Warsaw.
They knew how to go about those things.
So next morning, the first thing in the morning, we found ourselves out of the camp into a train-- not into first selection.
They selected the young people to go to work to Germany, and the older people and women with children on the train.
We still didn't know where we are going.
This is October?
That is October '44.
They sent us in those cattle trains-- but not covered.
They were covered trains, but it's open, at least.
And the same thing as the Jews, standing room only.
And they sent us south.
So south was Auschwitz.
So we did not know what they are going to do with us.
Yes.
They did not play around very much with Poles, but mostly after the losses that they-- their losses during the resistance, yes?
And they were very bitter.
They were losing the war then.
The Russians were on their doorstep.
So we didn't know what they're going to do.
They brought us to a small village-- south, north of Krakow-- [INAUDIBLE].
That time I remember because that was-- and there was a committee of the Poles, a committee to help the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Warsaw refugees.
First of all, they gave us something to eat.
That was the first thing.
And then I got a pair of wooden clogs because my sandals fell to pieces.
OK, I did not have anything else on the my feet, and they fell to pieces so I got the wooden clog.
And it was such a strong winter.
It was snow everywhere, knee deep.
And then they took the whole wagon-- not the whole transport because each part was sent somewhere else-- but we kept together, our 20 people.
And they put us on a lorry and sent us to a small village, a hamlet really, in the middle of big forest.
Poland had then, there was very big forest.
And we were lucky.
We got-- we went to different huts, between different peasant.
The couple with the small baby, and I with a baby, and my uncle, and the one old woman who lived in the same house on the upper floor, we got one hut.
And it was quite a nice one.
It was only one old woman.
And we were living there, the peasant were giving us something to eat.
I was knitting a little bit for them, so I got a bit something additional for the baby.
It was-- we were sitting there.
Then before Christmas that year, '44, my uncle got in touch with the family-- with her, Aunt Paula's family-- who was in a small village north, not far from Lodz.
That was nearly on the German border.
They were displaced from Lodz because that was connected to Germany, and as they did not want to sign the German citizenship.
As the Poles, they were thrown out, and they lived in this-- So we went there.
By train.
Very much.
The train was absolutely full.
First of all, I had to go walk 10 kilometers, in snow, with the baby to the railway station.
Then we came there and the train comes in.
It was like a [INAUDIBLE].
People were on the roof, on the steps, hanging from the windows.
And I stood there and I said, well, how I'm going to come in?

Then a door from a carriage opens, and the German in a uniform opens the door and tells me come in into a German wagon, beautifully lit, and there were perhaps five or six people in the whole-- warm, dry.
And I was sitting there next to a woman in a beautiful fur coat.
And she had all the food.
She had the thermos flask, and she started to feed the baby.
And so I come to my destination.
Who?
I will tell you, I-- sometimes I think myself, I must have looked very helpless.
I was very small, very thin at this time.
And like a man who was not far from me, he looked at me, and he told me, give me the baby, we'll help you.
A child carries a child, you know?
I was-- really I looked like a small child. back then.
They must have really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because I was really helped by so many people.
So, we came to this nation, we came to this place, and there started a bit of a time not when as she somehow, she looked at me and she disliked me.
One of the few people who really-- she took a dislike-- This is Auntie Paula's--
Cousin.
But she could not turn me out because I was the cousin.
And so we were there together.
Luckily, there was, in the neighboring room, was a young couple from Lodz, which had a baby-- a few months old baby-- and we got very friendly.
And they were really my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, how you tell?
Not only cover, but they gave me a bit of moral support.
Not did they knew that I am Jewish.
But anyway, we stayed there.
It was so cold that, for example, we had to have water in the bucket, in the room where we lived.
In the morning when I got up, I had to break the ice in the bucket with a axe now because it was-- everything was frozen stiff.
It was such a cold winter.
And anyway, we stayed there and then the Russians started to advance.
And as it was a German-populated area, all the peasant flew on the sign on that the Russians are coming, and we were left alone.
We had nothing even to eat.
There were a few rotten potatoes, a few rotten carrots in the cellar, and that was that and this time.
There were no peasant anymore where to-- even to get something.
So this young couple decided when the Russian took over Lodz, they decided to go back home, and they begged me to go with them.
But then, I had a glimmer that when the Russian are here, perhaps I will be able to go back to Szczecin, somehow.
So I told them, no, I will stay.
And my uncle went to Warsaw to see what's happened there.
So I stayed all alone with the baby in this abandoned, lonely hut.
But then he came back, and he told me-- How long were you alone?
I don't know, a few days.
There was also rotten potatoes and rotten-- you should have seen the baby Michael.
He just closed his eyes when he saw it.
He was a good eater.
But anyway, he--
